Title: CCM welcomes revised Selangor guidelines on non-Muslim houses of worship
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Council of Churches Malaysia (CCM) has welcomed the newly revised building guidelines for non-Muslim houses of worship in Selangor, which require large new township developments to allocate dedicated land for such facilities.
CCM said the updates reflected feedback from stakeholders and faith-based groups gathered during meetings with the state government.
It said now, developers of new projects with a minimum of 5,000 residents are required to surrender land the size of at least 1.5 acres to be designated as religious land for non-Muslim houses of worship.
"Developers are also required to provide sufficient access roads and other infrastructure, such as water and electricity," it said in a statement today.
The revised guidelines also officially permit commercial, industrial, and institutional properties to be used as places of worship.
According to CCM, the revised guidelines were presented during the third meeting between the Selangor State Executive Council (Exco) and leaders of various faith bodies on Monday (June 22).
The meeting was chaired by state exco member Ng Sze Han, who also serves as the co-chairperson of the Special Committee on Buddhist, Christian, Hindu, Sikh, and Tao Religious Affairs (Limas).
"Ng Sze Han informed the faith leaders that the state exco has kept to its promise to review and update the PlanMalaysia Selangor's Building Guidelines.
"This latest version incorporates all the feedback from stakeholders gathered from past discussions," CCM said in its statement.
Additionally, the revised guidelines offer greater flexibility regarding access road and setback requirements for religious buildings, subject to approval from local councils and Limas.
The Limas committee will also act as a mediator and arbitrator in the event of any disputes or contentions between property owners and local authorities.
The new guidelines will only apply to new developments, leaving existing houses of worship unaffected, the statement added.
"On behalf of its member churches and organizations, CCM extends our deep appreciation to the Selangor state exco for listening to the concerns of the non-Muslim communities by adopting these revised guidelines, which are more inclusive and fair," CCM said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Diplomacy with all countries secures long-term energy supply for transport, logistics – Loke
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Long-term supply agreements for oil, gas and diesel lasting at least 20 years will provide stability to Malaysia's logistics and transport sectors, says Transport Minister Anthony Loke.
He said energy security was essential for both sectors, which depend on a steady and predictable supply to ensure uninterrupted operations, adding that the government was prioritising supply stability amid shifting global conditions, supported by Malaysia's neutral foreign policy and engagement with major powers.
"We take a bold approach in diplomacy with all countries, particularly major powers such as Russia, the United States and China. This helps ensure access to sufficient and continuous energy resources," he said at the Global Maritime Economy Conference 2026.
Earlier, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said strategic cooperation with Russia could help secure Malaysia's long-term energy needs, adding that Russian President Vladimir Putin had indicated readiness for a 20-year oil, gas and diesel supply arrangement.
On the impact of a reported 60-day sanctions relief involving Iranian crude oil, Loke said any easing of restrictions could increase global supply and help stabilise prices, but cautioned that the situation remained fluid.
"This development is seen as positive as it provides an opportunity to diversify oil supply sources. However, the agreement remains uncertain and subject to change," he said.
He said the government was therefore pursuing a diversified energy sourcing strategy to safeguard economic activity.
On the aviation sector, Loke said Malaysian airlines had remained resilient despite higher operating costs and a marginal dip in flight numbers, adding that the impact was minimal compared with other countries where services had been suspended on several routes.
He added that preliminary discussions had been held with airlines, stressing that aviation remains closely tied to tourism and business activity.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MARKET PULSE PM JUNE 23, 2026 [WATCH]
Article:

Title: Lazada cuts jobs by 5.0pct amid rising competition: Report
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Lazada has retrenched about five per cent of its workforce across Southeast Asia amid growing competition and a broader push towards operational efficiency.
According to a report by the Straits Times, the layoffs affect employees across the region, including an undisclosed number of staff in Singapore, where affected workers were notified on June 23.
A Lazada spokesperson told the media that the company was reviewing selected roles across Southeast Asia.
"Where roles are affected, employees will be engaged directly and with care, respect and dignity," the spokesperson was quoted as saying.
The retrenchment comes amid wider restructuring across the e-commerce sector.
Earlier this month, rival Shopee reportedly cut hundreds of developer jobs globally, while Amazon also announced job reductions in Singapore as it shifts its focus from local grocery fulfillment to international product shipments.
Meanwhile, Derrick Teo, chief executive officer of manpower consultancy firm Elitez Group, told the Straits Times that while the e-commerce industry continues to grow, the era of rapid hiring and growth-at-all-costs strategies has ended.
"Technology and artificial intelligence are helping companies operate more efficiently, meaning business growth may no longer produce the same level of headcount growth seen during the pandemic," he said.
The Food, Drinks and Allied Workers Union (FDAWU) general-secretary Sankaradass S. Chami said Lazada had informed the union in advance about the job cuts.
"This advance notification reflects the positive labour-management relationship and trust built over time," he said, adding that Lazada will provide one year of union membership and training grant support for affected union members.
The Taskforce for Responsible Retrenchment and Employment Facilitation also noted that Lazada had notified the task force about its restructuring exercise, which is part of a larger organisational realignment.
Lazada previously laid off an undisclosed number of staff in January 2024 without consulting the FDAWU, which it is unionised under.
The union had expressed its disappointment then, and the matter was escalated to the Ministry of Manpower.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Onn Hafiz allegedly barred from entering Felda scheme for land title event
Article:
KLUANG: Caretaker Johor Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi claims he was barred from entering a Felda settlement to present land titles to settlers two days ago.
He said the incident forced the Johor Felda Settlers Day 2026 celebration, originally scheduled to take place at Felda Ulu Belitong on June 20 and 21, to be relocated to Dewan Dato Onn in Taman Sri Lambak here.
Despite the setback, Onn Hafiz expressed gratitude that the land title presentation could still take place today.
"I am deeply grateful this afternoon. Thank God because even though we were supposed to meet two days ago, and we were tested, blocked, and pressured, by the will of God, we still met despite the slight delay.
"I have been looking forward to meeting a group that the state government considers its golden child. Felda is Johor's golden child.
"I wanted to meet Felda settlers across all 73 land schemes, including friends in the Sembrong parliamentary constituency, Felda Bukit Tongkat and Felda Ulu Belitong.
"As it turns out, I was not even allowed to enter the Felda settlement, even though it was just to hand over the land titles to the settlers.
"For the first time in my life in Johor, a menteri besar is not allowed into a felda area," he said during his speech at the Felda settlers land title presentation ceremony held at Dewan Dato Onn, Sembrong parliament community house.
Also present was caretaker Johor Agriculture, Agro-based Industry and Rural Development Committee chairman Datuk Zahari Sarip.
Onn Hafiz, however, clarified that he did not blame the Felda management for the complications.
"I want to give an assurance to the mothers and fathers in this hall that no matter how much I am pressured, blocked, insulted, or criticised, it will not diminish or dampen my spirit to fight for the Felda community.
"Many here have been victimised, and InsyaAllah, the prayers of the victimised are answered. In Johor, we have the Johor Way, and no matter what they do, it does not bother us.
"The more they do this to us, the more we will rise. Why? Because what we are fighting for is our religion, race, and homeland," he said.
Earlier, Zahari apologised to Felda settlers after the celebration at Felda Ulu Belitong was cancelled, noting that the decision did not originate from the state government or the settlers.
He said the state government was only informed that Johor Felda had received directives not to proceed with the event.
The directive was received just four days before the programme, despite extensive joint preparations made by the state government, Felda and local scheme committees.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Drug-positive driver jailed after ramming police ASP at roadblock
Article:
KUCHING: A 29-year-old local man was sentenced to jail after being found guilty of ramming a roadblock and hitting a senior police officer on duty during an integrated crime prevention operation at Jalan Padungan here on Saturday.
Kuching district police chief ACP Alexson Naga Chabu said the accused was arraigned in the Kuching Court yesterday and sentenced to six months' jail under Section 26(2) of the Police Act 1967, one year's jail under Section 332 of the Penal Code, and nine months' jail under Section 186 of the same Code.
"In the incident at about 2.40am on June 20, the suspect rammed the roadblock before hitting an ASP-ranked senior police officer who was on duty, causing injuries to the victim's waist and right elbow.
"The suspect fled the scene before being arrested around 7am the same day at Jalan Bintara, Samariang, following intelligence and further police action," he said in a statement today.
He said preliminary urine screening found the suspect positive for amphetamine, methamphetamine, ketamine, and benzodiazepine, while further checks revealed that he had six prior criminal records for various offences.
Alexson said the suspect will also be charged under Section 44(1A)(a) of the Road Transport Act 1987 at a later date.
He advised the public to comply with roadblock instructions and not to drive under the influence of drugs or alcohol for the safety of road users and enforcement personnel.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: New York Mayor Mamdani endorsements test Democrats' appetite for far-left candidates
Article:
NEW York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani's push to remake the Democratic Party into a democratic socialist powerhouse faces a primary election test on Tuesday.
But win or lose, it is unlikely to provide an effective blueprint for Democrats taking control of the US Congress in November or the White House in 2028.
The mayor, who shocked the political world with his 2025 election, is backing a slate of fellow democratic socialist candidates running in Tuesday's New York primaries as they take on establishment Democrats, including one senior Latino in the US House of Representatives.
This comes on the heels of democratic socialist candidates winning primaries in Washington, D.C, and Los Angeles mayor races. Furthermore, a democratic socialist won the Seattle mayor's race last year.
Mamdani's efforts to expand the democratic socialist base in the US comes after a decade-long effort that was spurred on by Senator Bernie Sanders' surprisingly popular 2016 presidential campaign and his efforts to nurture a new generation of democratic socialist leaders.
But it is also in response to progressive Democratic voters' anger at President Donald Trump's agenda and governing style, and at the Biden administration's backing of Israel's war against Gaza following a Hamas attack.
Israel's response resulted in more than 73,000 Palestinian deaths.
"Energy on the far right ignites energy on the far left. Politics is reactive," said Steve Israel, a former US House member from New York who late in his congressional career ran an operation to elect more Democrats.
For months after Mamdani won his 2025 primary election, House Democratic Leader Hakeem Jeffries was dogged by reporters asking whether he would endorse his fellow New Yorker.
Jeffries did so, but kept the everyone guessing until just 11 days before the general election.
Meanwhile, Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer of New York remained silent on Mamdani throughout the campaign.
The rub is that Jeffries is positioned to ascend to US House speaker and thus second in line for the presidency if Democrats win November's midterm elections.
The path to victory does not run through "blue," solidly Democratic congressional districts. Instead, it's the "purple" swing districts where Democrats need to beat Republicans.
Nonetheless, if the Mamdani-backed democratic socialist Darializa Avila Chevalier defeats five-term Democratic Representative Adriano Espaillat in New York City's 13th district covering northern Manhattan and the Bronx, it would carry national implications that could complicate Jeffries' task.
"If a DSA member could knock off the chair of the Congressional Hispanic Caucus, that could matter," said Matt Bennett, co-founder of Third Way, a centrist Democratic consultancy.
[[nid:1470807]]
Even more relevant could be the controversial stances Avila Chevalier has touted in past social media postings, such as calling for abolishing police and border controls and raising questions about Israel's right to exist.
"This is precisely the kind of person that they (Republicans) love to use to weaponise against other Democrats" running for office in competitive races, Bennett said.
Steve Israel agreed and said in an interview: "I do worry that the strength of democratic socialists in places like New York and California will be misread as the center of gravity for Democrats across the country" this November or in the 2028 presidential election.
Avila Chevalier has since deleted her social media posts and apologised for some of the language she used.
But in a Thursday interview with a consortium of editors on June 17, Avila Chevalier said: "I think that we just should not have a system that allows (migrant) deportation to happen at all," saying it "is rooted in deeply racist ideology."
In response to her views, Espaillat said in a June 16 posting on X: "We can't just sweep things under the rug. Darializa has taken very extreme positions as reflected in her comments on social media not too long ago."
He added, "She is unfit for office and voters are smart enough to see that."
A Democratic socialist is also attempting to defeat incumbent Democratic Representative Dan Goldman in New York City's 10th congressional district, while another is vying for the 7th congressional district House seat where Democratic Representative Nydia Velazquez is retiring.
Alex Jacquez, a progressive strategist who was a senior adviser to Sanders' 2020 presidential campaign, said in an interview that focus groups and opinion polls deliver the message that Democratic voters' level of dissatisfaction with their leaders runs deep.
"That is really where you are seeing the fault lines. Are you willing to take on the wealthy and take on corporations and take on the status quo to deliver results. Or are you not," he said of the populist message democratic socialists are trying to win on this fall and through the 2028 elections and beyond.
Meantime, outside of deep-blue districts of New York, California and other Democratic strongholds, the party is running women with strong military backgrounds in places like Florida and Colorado for example.
"Most of the competitive districts for Democrats are red and pink districts that you can only win as a Democrat in ... where more moderate stances resonate in races against incumbent Republicans," Israel said.
He added that presidential elections are not won in blue states. "It's won in seven moderate battleground states."
* The writer is from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ex-Melaka officer charged with fraud and cheating exco members
Article:
MELAKA: A former senior state civil servant was hauled to the Ayer Keroh Sessions Court today to face seven counts of misappropriation of funds and cheating involving more than RM60,000.
D. Puhnitha Devi, 43, the former Assistant Senior State Secretary of the Corporate Communications Division at the Melaka Chief Minister's Department, pleaded not guilty to all charges before Judge Elesabet Paya Wan.
On the first three counts, Puhnitha Devi was charged with dishonest misappropriation of funds.
She allegedly embezzled funds belonging to the Melaka State Administrative and Diplomatic Officers Association, alongside donation funds meant for staff working at the 2024 Melaka state-level Madani Aidilfitri celebration.
The transactions, totalling RM56,600, were allegedly carried out on March 24 and April 7, 2022, at the Ayer Keroh Bank Islam branch.
The charges were framed under Section 403 of the Penal Code, which carries a maximum jail term of five years, whipping and a fine upon conviction.
Puhnitha also faced four separate counts of cheating three people, including two state executive council members.
She had allegedly deceived them into making financial contributions for a family day and an annual work target event that never took place.
She was charged under Section 420 of the Penal Code, which provides for a prison sentence of up to 10 years, whipping and a fine if found guilty.
Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) deputy public prosecutor Sharina Farhana Nor Sa'ari applied for bail to be set at RM30,000 with one surety.
She also requested the court impose additional conditions, requiring the accused to surrender her passport and report to the MACC office once a month until the case is disposed of.
Puhnitha's defence counsel Intan Nur Alia Nodzri pleaded for a lower bail amount, telling the court that her client had been unemployed for a year.
"She is currently caring for her ailing parents, and the family is solely dependent on financial aid from her younger brother," she said.
Judge Elesabet Paya Wan granted bail of RM15,000 with one surety and allowed the prosecution's request for monthly reporting to MACC and for her passport to be impounded.
The court fixed Aug 3 for mention and submission of documents.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US, Israeli relations under strain as Israelis worry over Iran deal
Article:
TEL AVIV: American allies of President Donald Trump this week defended him to an Israeli public anxious about a US interim deal with Iran and White House criticism that together appeared to signal fissures in Israel's decades-old alliance with Washington.
The US-Israeli relationship has been on a roller coaster, from the early confidence they shared after their joint attack on Iran to public disagreements between Trump and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu over how to end the four-month-old war.
Netanyahu and many other Israelis see a risk that Trump's memorandum of understanding with Iran will empower a state they regard as their deadliest enemy and constrict their ability to respond to threats from Iran-backed Hizbollah in Lebanon.
They sense the US alliance - long the bedrock of Israel's strategic approach - is under strain as opinion polls show Americans increasingly unhappy with Israel and their strongest champion in Washington appears to be turning away.
"The United States and Israel have an unbreakable bond," Mike Huckabee, the US ambassador to Israel, said on Sunday after acknowledging there was an "enormous level of anxiety about the relationship."
He spoke at a foreign policy conference in Jerusalem where concerns about the state of the U.S.-Israel alliance dominated many of the discussions.
Mark Levin, a conservative Fox News commentator and longtime Trump supporter who has broken with the president over the Iran deal, told the audience that while he did not like the agreement and believed that the "Iranian regime" had to be destroyed, he nevertheless praised Trump for what he said was the president's support for liberty, religious freedom, Christianity and Judaism.
Alongside their concerns about the wording of the Iran deal, Israelis worry about Trump's insistence on Israel agreeing a ceasefire with Hizbollah in Lebanon and his language responding to Netanyahu's resistance to those agreements.
In recent weeks Trump has called Netanyahu "fucking crazy," lectured Israel that "you don't have to knock an apartment down every time you're looking for somebody" and publicly pondered asking Syria to replace Israeli troops in Lebanon.
Vice President JD Vance also struck a more critical tone, saying "Trump is the only head of state in the entire world who is sympathetic to the nation of Israel at this moment in time," adding later that not all criticism of Israel should be dismissed as antisemitism.
The fact such sharp views are emanating from Trump's Republican Party is especially worrying for many Israelis, with US Democrats far more vocally critical of Israel than in previous years.
Sid Rosenberg, a prominent conservative New York radio host, told Israelis that for all their concerns about Trump, he was the best option for them.
"You could have JD Vance. Good luck with that," he said, after acknowledging that "a lot of people in Israel are very, very upset" with the president.
While large majorities of Republicans 50 and older view Israel positively, younger conservative Americans have grown more critical, a Pew Research Center poll from late March showed.
Some 57 per cent of Republicans aged 18-49 have an unfavourable opinion of Israel, up from 50 per cent a year previously.
Many Americans, including prominent Democratic politicians, were outraged by the scale of death and devastation in Israel's military campaign in Gaza after the deadly Hamas attack of Oct 7, 2023, on Israeli communities and the taking of hostages.
Israel has also faced criticism over the joint decision to launch the war on Iran, a conflict that is deeply unpopular in the United States, including among Trump's conservative base.
Victoria Coates, vice president at the conservative Heritage Foundation think tank and Trump's deputy national security adviser during his first term, suggested on Monday that the US-Israeli relationship was strained but expressed confidence that the leaders of both countries would bring it "back on track."
A day earlier, speaking at the conference, she had said that recent days had been "challenging for all of us, to put it mildly," but that there had been plenty of "great and good things" in Trump's second term "for which we can and should be grateful."
Until recently, Trump had been seen in Israel as its strongest-ever White House ally after his decision in his first term to recognise Jerusalem as Israel's capital and Israeli sovereignty over the occupied Golan Heights and his leading diplomatic role securing the release of hostages last year.
Two Israeli officials familiar with Netanyahu's thinking said the prime minister was not concerned that comments by Trump and Vance indicated any meaningful US policy changes such as slower arms deliveries.
Netanyahu believed the comments might be partly geared towards assuaging voters ahead of US midterm elections in November amid growing frustration over Israel and the war, said the officials who spoke on condition of anonymity.
The anxiety in Israel has led some prominent figures to say it is time for the country to envisage a future without strong US support and to further build up its own military and technological capabilities.
Ohad Tal, chair of the US-Israel caucus in Israel's parliament the Knesset, said Israelis needed to prepare for the day when there is a less supportive US president "and this is why we have to be much more independent and we have to forge new alliances."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bill seeks to expand law on child sexual offences committed abroad
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Sexual Offences against Children (Amendment) Bill 2026, which seeks to broaden the extraterritorial reach of Malaysia's child sexual offence laws, was tabled for its first reading in the Dewan Rakyat today.
Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said, when tabling the bill, said the second and third readings would be presented within the current parliamentary sitting, which runs until July 16.
Based on the blue bill book, the proposed amendments aim to strengthen the Sexual Offences against Children Act 2017 by expanding its application to offences committed outside Malaysia by individuals with a connection to Malaysia.
Under the current law, the Act applies only to offences committed abroad by Malaysian citizens against any child.
The amendment proposes extending its reach to include offences committed overseas by Malaysian permanent residents and persons who have their habitual residence in Malaysia against any child.
It would allow authorities to take action against any person who commits such offences outside Malaysia against a child who is a Malaysian citizen, a permanent resident or a person habitually resident in Malaysia.
Such offences may be dealt with as if they had been committed within Malaysia.
The amendments to the Act will not incur any additional financial expenditure for the government.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Prisons Bill proposes electronic prisoner tagging, volunteer rehab scheme
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Prisons (Amendment) Bill 2026, which proposes the use of electronic monitoring devices for prisoners and the introduction of volunteers to support rehabilitation programmes, was tabled for its first reading in the Dewan Rakyat today.
Deputy Home Minister Datuk Seri Dr Shamsul Anuar Nasarah, when tabling the bill, said the second reading is expected to be presented during the current parliamentary sitting.
According to the Blue Bill Book, the proposed new Section 66A seeks to empower the Prisons Commissioner-General to appoint volunteers to assist prison officers in conducting rehabilitation programmes under the Prisons Act 1995.
Under the proposal, appointed volunteers may be paid allowances as determined by the minister in consultation with the finance minister.
However, they will not be entitled to remuneration and will be deemed public servants under the Penal Code while performing their duties.
The bill also proposes a new provision empowering the Commissioner-General to order the installation of electronic monitoring devices on any prisoner, including those in custody or those released on licence or parole.
It would also introduce offences for non-compliance with the terms and conditions of electronic monitoring, as well as for tampering with, damaging, destroying or losing such devices.
Any person convicted of tampering with, damaging, destroying or losing an electronic monitoring device may be jailed for up to three years and ordered to pay compensation for any damage or loss.
The court may also impose an additional prison term of up to three years in default of payment.
The bill also seeks to raise the general penalty under Act 537 or its regulations, where no specific penalty is provided, increasing the maximum fine from RM500 to RM5,000 and the maximum jail term from six months to one year.
Among other proposed changes is the creation of new ranks, including Assistant Superintendent of Prisons for senior officers and Chief Sub-Inspector of Prisons as the highest rank for junior officers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Don't force children into boarding schools, says Mara chairman
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Parents should not force their children to enrol in boarding schools, including Maktab Rendah Sains Mara (MRSM), if they show no interest, said Datuk Dr Asyraf Wajdi Dusuki.
The Majlis Amanah Rakyat (Mara) chairman warned that such coercion could severely impact a child's emotional well-being and development.
"Do not force them if the child has no interest in entering boarding schools, including MRSM.
"It can affect the child's emotions and trigger a rebellious attitude, which could eventually lead to inappropriate behaviour," he said in a Facebook post today.
Last week, Asyraf said that the Besut and Balik Pulau MRSM campuses recorded a drastic drop in student disciplinary issues and near-zero bullying cases after hiring former military personnel as full-time wardens in a pilot project.
The initiative is set to
expand to al
l 58 MRSM institutions nationwide.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tok Mat: Fund for Palestine audited, money given to UN, Red Crescent
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Money collected via the Humanitarian Trust Fund for the People of Palestine is audited and distributed to international organisations operating in the war-torn area, the Foreign Ministry said.
Its minister, Datuk Seri Mohamad Hasan, said the trust fund was managed transparently and in accordance with established procedures.
He said it is chaired by the ministry's secretary-general, supported by a dedicated committee and finance division, and is audited by the Auditor-General, with regular reporting to the cabinet.
"Our NGO is Ops Ihsan, which was established by the ministry and has 38 affiliated bodies, including Mercy Malaysia and we ensure that aid really reaches there.
"That is why we give it directly to the United Nations Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees, United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs, the Red Crescent there and Mercy.
"So, it is not true what is being said on social media, saying that we give arbitrarily to NGOs," he told the Dewan Rakyat.
Mohamad, commonly known as Tok Mat, was responding to a question from Ahmad Tarmizi Sulaiman (PN–Sik) on the governance of the fund following concerns raised on social media.
He said food and medical aid from Malaysia has begun entering through land routes, but it is currently limited to around 100 containers per day.
"A lot of our goods have already entered through our friends in Egypt."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Highway 'race' involving Hilux and Viva under police investigation
Article:
NIBONG TEBAL: Police are investigating a viral video showing two vehicles allegedly being driven recklessly on the North-South Expressway in Seberang Perai Selatan here.
District deputy police chief Deputy Superintendent Mohd Norazmi Abd Ghapar said police detected the two-minute and 43-second video on Facebook at about 8.30pm yesterday.
The video, uploaded by the Facebook account "Penang Kini", purportedly showed a Perodua Viva and a Toyota Hilux
being driven dangerously
, appearing to race on the North-South Expressway from Jawi towards Bukit Tambun.
"The manner of driving posed a danger not only to the motorists involved but also to other road users.
"The case is being investigated under Section 42(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987 for reckless and dangerous driving," he said in a statement today.
Mohd Norazmi urged members of the public with information on the incident to contact the district police headquarters at 04-585 8222.
He also reminded motorists that dangerous driving could lead to fatal accidents, and stern legal action would be taken against offenders.
He said the district police would continue to intensify traffic enforcement operations, including Op Mabuk, to ensure road safety.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Russia says US drifting from role as 'objective mediator' in Ukraine war
Article:
MOSCOW: Russia on Tuesday said the United States was no longer trying to be an "objective mediator" in its efforts to broker an end to the Ukraine war, launched by Moscow's offensive in 2022.
US-led talks on ending Europe's deadliest conflict since World War II remain effectively frozen as President Donald Trump has shifted his attention towards the Middle East after ordering strikes on Iran in late February.
But during a summit in France earlier this month, G7 leaders – including the US president – agreed on a final statement that included references to the Ukraine war, in contrast to last year's meeting, when Trump walked out early.
As well as increasing supplies of air defence equipment to Ukraine, the leaders agreed to increase the pressure on the Russian "war economy" by strengthening sanctions, including on Moscow's fossil fuel revenues.
"As for the United States, judging by their actions, they appear to be abandoning any claim to the role of an objective mediator and are instead pursuing a course of escalating sanctions pressure on Russia," Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov told foreign envoys in Moscow on Tuesday.
Lavrov also said that by providing military support to Ukraine, "Europe is once again becoming a major threat to international peace and security."
Since coming back to the White House in 2025, Trump – who had pledged he would end the Ukraine war within a day of taking office – has been pushing both sides to engage in negotiations.
There has been little progress in the US-brokered shuttle diplomacy between the neighbours, with Kyiv refusing to give in to Moscow's maximalist demands and Russia refusing to drop them.
Russia, which launched its offensive in 2022, has demanded Ukraine pull out of its eastern Donbas region – parts of which Kyiv's army still controls – as a precondition to peace talks.
Ukraine ruled it out, saying such a move would only embolden Moscow.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Digital mob mentality' targeting refugees becoming a real-world problem, say groups
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A surge in algorithm-amplified online hate targeting refugees is fuelling a "digital mob mentality" and driving real-world harassment, evictions and growing fear among vulnerable communities.
The Communications and Multimedia Content Forum of Malaysia has urged social media platforms to address algorithms pushing rage-bait content that repeatedly fuels hostility towards refugees and other vulnerable communities.
Its chief executive officer, Mediha Mahmood, said the growing trend risks creating a "digital mob mentality" that turns vulnerable groups, including children, into targets for online engagement.
She said platforms must also go beyond removing illegal content and address systems that reward harmful engagement.
"If harmful content is repeatedly amplified because it performs well, platforms must respond to that risk," she said.
Labelling content as pranks or social experiments, said Mediha, does not remove the responsibility to treat people with dignity.
"The concern is when vulnerable communities, especially children, are turned into props for reactions, ridicule or engagement — when their vulnerability itself becomes the source of entertainment.
"The line has been crossed when people become tools for provocation, humiliation or monetisation," she added.
She said repeated exposure to rage-bait content through algorithm-driven feeds can reinforce distorted perceptions over time, as users begin to see harmful or prejudiced views as normal and widely accepted.
"The scariest risk is that this digital mob mentality could cross over into real-life mobs that seek to harm others," she said.
On maintaining responsibility, Mediha said creators must consider whether content would still be acceptable if the persons involved were their own family members.
She also said audiences play a role, noting that engagement helps spread such content even when meant as criticism, and encouraged users to report harmful material instead of sharing it.
While Malaysia has laws and content standards, she said many harmful cases exist in a grey area that is not clearly illegal.
She called for a combined approach involving regulation, platform accountability and digital literacy.
"Creators shape the content, platforms shape the incentives, and audiences shape the demand. The real test is whether content respects human dignity," she added.
North-South Initiative executive director Adrian Pereira said online vigilante behaviour, including harassment, doxxing and targeting of community spaces has escalated to an unprecedented level.
This, he said, include school closures, evictions, job losses and disruptions to community programmes.
He said landlords have cancelled housing contracts, while refugees have been forced out of their homes.
In some cases, he said, community initiatives have also been put on hold due to safety concerns.
He said sections of the public spreading unverified narratives about refugees must be held accountable.
Instead of seeking clarity, he said such actions contributed to growing misunderstanding and hostility, "rather than helping Malaysians recognise that refugees are not the enemy".
"People have lost jobs, community members, refugees have lost jobs," he said.
Pereira added that many refugees have begun self-censoring out of fear their comments could be taken out of context.
He claimed the surge in hostility as a "manufactured hate campaign" driving attacks on marginalised communities, particularly Rohingya refugees.
The
New Straits Times
reached out to the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission for comment but has yet to receive a response.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UN chief urges AI firms to 'come clean' over environmental footprint
Article:
PARIS: UN chief Antonio Guterres called Tuesday for faster action on global warming, challenging AI firms to "come clean" about their environmental footprint and warning that fossil fuels were driving climate and energy crises.
As Europe bakes under a second heatwave in as many months, Guterres delivered a speech in London that painted a stark picture of a planet that has just endured its 11 hottest years on record.
"Climate chaos is accelerating before our eyes," Guterres said, while the energy crisis, fuelled by war in the Middle East, is "exposing the folly of a world hooked on hydrocarbons."
"It is clear that our world is facing a Tale of Two Crises," Guterres said, referencing the 19th century British writer Charles Dickens' "A Tale of Two Cities."
"On the surface, these crises may seem separate. But they share the same destructive origin: Fossil fuels," he said at London Climate Action Week, an annual gathering of policymakers, company executives and NGOs.
Guterres announced new initiatives to combat methane emissions and address concerns over the environmental footprint of energy-hungry data centres.
The growing energy, water and land use of data centres – vast server warehouses powering AI and other digital services – is putting pressure on local communities and the environment.
A UN study earlier this month found that the facilities consumed more electricity than all but 10 countries in 2025.
By 2030, they could use more power than all but five countries, the study found.
Guterres launched an AI Environmental Transparency Initiative, urging every major artificial intelligence company to measure and publicly disclose their environmental impact as well as commit to powering every data centre with renewable energy by 2030.
"It is time to come clean," Guterres said. "If AI is to help build a better future, it must be honest about what it costs us now."
Guterres warned that the world was "dangerously" off track in efforts to reach the global goal of achieving net zero emissions by 2050.
Countries agreed to pursue efforts to limit warming to 1.5C above pre-industrial levels under the 2015 Paris Agreement, but scientists now say that threshold could be breached by about 2030.
"We must act with far greater urgency to strictly limit the magnitude and duration of any overshoot beyond 1.5C," Guterres said.
Rising temperatures are pushing the world closer to "catastrophic tipping points", he said.
The United Nations Scientific Advisory Board released a report outlining the dangers of crossing irreversible tipping points, from ice melt that would further raise sea levels to the collapse of coral reefs and Amazon decline.
The UN chief's warning came as Europe's latest heatwave brought record temperatures in France and seared other European countries this week.
Guterres called for a rapid cut in CO2 emissions from oil, gas and coal – the main driver of long-term warming, which remains in the atmosphere for centuries.
He urged governments to tax the windfall profits of oil and gas giants and said the world has a "clean way out" of fossil fuels through accelerating the transition to cheaper renewable energy.
The UN chief also called for renewed efforts to reduce methane emissions, which account for one-third of warming and are about 80 times more potent than CO2 but break down in the atmosphere in a decade or two.
Guterres said the agriculture and waste sectors must take steps to curb their methane output but he put a "special focus" on the fossil fuel industry to "do what is long overdue."
Around 70 per cent of oil and gas methane emissions can be eliminated with existing technology, but some 167 billion cubic metres of gas were flared in 2025 alone, as much as Africa consumes in a year, he said.
He called on governments to set a "new global standard" for the oil and gas sector that would lead to "near-zero" methane emissions.
"This is indeed the best of times and the worst of times. The worst because climate impacts are intensifying, tipping points are looming, and the energy crisis has exposed the deep risks of dependence on fossil fuels," he said.
"But also the best because the renewables revolution is well underway."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Taman Negara's Seberang Ara canopy walkway reopens after repairs
Article:
JERANTUT: Taman Negara's popular 700m Seberang Ara canopy walkway has reopened to visitors after being closed for more than a year due to structural damage caused by fallen trees.
The 700m-long walkway, one of the park's most popular attractions, had remained closed for repair works since February last year.
Tahan assemblyman Mohd Zakhwan Ahmad Badarddin said he took part in a simulation exercise to test the canopy walkway on June 18 before it was officially reopened to the public yesterday.
"The simulation exercise, which began on June 17 and ended on June 21, was conducted to ensure that all aspects of safety, operations and visitor comfort met the highest standards before the reopening.
"The canopy walkway is one of the main attractions for visitors to the national park. Visitors can now enjoy views of the jungle floor from a unique vantage point," he told the New Straits Times.
He said the experience of walking on the canopy walkway should not be seen as a test of courage, but rather as an opportunity to appreciate nature and the invaluable natural heritage of Taman Negara.
In a Facebook post, Zakhwan said the Seberang Ara canopy walkway, built about 30 to 40m above the ground, consists of 16 suspension bridges and 17 platforms, with the entire route taking between 45 minutes and one hour to complete.
He hoped the reopening of the walkway would attract more local and international visitors while generating economic spillover benefits for the community.
The three-decade-old 530m canopy walkway at Teresek Hill in the national park remains closed following a freak storm in May last year, which damaged a main supporting tree that later died.
Last year,
Taman Negara
tour guides expressed frustration over the prolonged closure of both canopy walkways without any indication of when they would reopen.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israel's 'deliberate targeting' of children part of ongoing Gaza 'genocide': UN probe
Article:
GENEVA: Israel is deliberately targeting Palestinian children in what has become a key factor in an ongoing "genocide" in Gaza, United Nations investigators charged on Tuesday, in a report slammed by Israel.
The UN Independent International Commission of Inquiry said it had found evidence that "Palestinian children have been deliberately targeted and killed by Israeli security forces."
This, it said, was a key factor in establishing "the genocidal intent of the Israeli authorities and security forces to destroy the larger Palestinian group in Gaza."
The three-member investigative team, which does not speak for the UN itself, first determined in a report last September that Israel had committed "genocide" in the war in Gaza – a finding Israel flatly rejected.
In Tuesday's follow-up report, they said the intense scale and systematic nature of Israeli military operations had continued, resulting in the "unprecedented" death, injury and trauma of Palestinian children.
There were "reasonable grounds" to conclude that Israel's authorities and security forces "have continued to commit the crime of genocide" in Gaza, they said.
Israel, which has long been harshly critical of the commission, slammed the report as "defamatory" and a "libellous sham."
It accused the investigators of ignoring "the brutal tactics of Hamas, which ruthlessly attacks Israeli children and uses Palestinian children as human shields."
Childhood 'erased' in Gaza
The commission, which was established by the UN Human Rights Council in 2021, examined for its latest report crimes affecting Palestinian children, and how living conditions imposed by Israel in Gaza were "resulting in preventable mortality of children."
"Israeli authorities and security forces have deliberately targeted Palestinian children resulting in genocide, crimes against humanity and war crimes in the Gaza Strip, and war crimes in the West Bank," the team said in a statement.
The commission said that severe physical and mental injuries, mass trauma, orphanhood, separation, disability, repeated displacements, starvation, and the collapse of education and healthcare had "erased childhood" in Gaza and would continue to affect the territory's children throughout their lives.
"By targeting children, Israel is attacking the very capacity of the Palestinian people to exist and to determine their future," said Indian judge Srinivasan Muralidhar, who chairs the inquiry.
"Even after the October 2025 ceasefire, children continue to be killed and seriously injured."
'Strategy to destroy'
The report comes days after the UN children's agency Unicef said at least 265 children had been killed and hundreds more wounded in Gaza since the ceasefire between Israel and Hamas took effect.
[[nid:1470755]]
Unicef said children had been shot, bombed and struck by quadcopters, killed in tents, in schools and while playing football or fishing.
The Hamas Oct 7, 2023 attack on Israel resulted in the deaths of 1,221 people, according to an AFP tally based on official Israeli figures.
Israel's retaliatory response in Gaza has killed more than 72,800 people, according to the territory's health ministry.
The UN inquiry said that during the first two years of the war at least 20,179 children were killed and 44,143 injured "as a direct result of the hostilities in Gaza."
The killing and maiming of Palestinian children "was part of a strategy to destroy the biological continuity and future existence of the Palestinian group in Gaza", it said.
Disability a 'demographic reality'
By targeting children, the report said, "Israel is eroding the foundational structure of Palestinian society, weakening the demographic vitality."
Israel was responsible for causing a "severe orphan crisis", while wounded youngsters "face a lifetime of disability" – now "a defining demographic reality" among Gaza's children, it said.
The siege of Gaza "directly undermined reproductive and newborn health", while the collapse of public health programmes "eroded the conditions necessary for a healthy next generation."
The report listed Israeli divisions, brigades and units that may be responsible for killing children, in specific incidents in Gaza and the West Bank.
Besides Gaza, the commission also documented a sharp increase in violence by Israeli settlers against Palestinian children in the West Bank, which Israel has occupied since 1967.
The commission urged all UN member states, including Israel, to ensure accountability for crimes committed.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 13 plead not guilty to rioting in Jinjang Utara
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Thirteen men, including two teenagers, pleaded not guilty at the Magistrate's Court to rioting with dangerous weapons in Jinjang Utara two weeks ago.
T. Kavilan, 25, Wong Kui Yoong, 51, Leow Mun Yee, 33, Leong Chun Kit, 26, Yam Kit Lun, 21, Lim Zhen Huang, 31, Tan Kai Hao, 30, Teh Jin Huat, 40, Wong Kai Yan, 24, Lim Eng Hoon, 24, Kong Wai Lun, 22, Tan Hwa Yuan, 19, and a 17-year-old entered their pleas before Magistrate Illi Marisqa Khalizan.
They were jointly charged with rioting while armed with dangerous weapons, namely rattan canes, alongside five others who are still at large.
The offence allegedly took place at 3.27pm in front of an electrical shop in Jalan Jinjang, Jinjang Utara, on June 11.
The charge was framed under Section 148 of the Penal Code, read together with Section 34, which carries a maximum five-year jail term, a fine, or both upon conviction.
Deputy Public Prosecutor Iman Nurhidayah Ezani did not offer bail, citing it as a non-bailable offence, and requested a date for the submission of documents.
However, defence lawyers Nurul Ashiqeen Mohd Sultan and Mona Leonard Lubon urged the court to use its discretion to grant bail.
They argued that their clients had cooperated with police investigations, held permanent employment, and posed no flight risk.
Illi Marisqa allowed bail of RM4,500 with one surety each for Yam, Hwa Yuan, and the 17-year-old while the remaining accused were granted bail of RM5,500 with one surety each.
The court fixed Aug 13 for the submission of documents.
Separately at the Sessions Court, Kong pleaded not guilty to a charge of voluntarily causing grievous hurt to the 17-year-old using a baseball bat, nearly two hours after the alleged rioting incident.
He was accused of committing the offence at 4.50pm on the same day at the same location.
The charge, framed under Section 326 of the Penal Code, carries a maximum 20-year jail term, a fine, or whipping upon conviction.
Judge Suhaila Haron granted Kong bail of RM8,000 with one surety, and ordered him to surrender his passport and report to a police station once a month.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hamzah: Wawasan Negara will strengthen PN's reform agenda
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Wawasan Negara's participation will add value to Perikatan Nasional's strength, said Opposition Leader Datuk Seri Hamzah Zainudin.
Hamzah said Parti Cinta Malaysia's (PCM) special delegates' meeting on Saturday had agreed to rebrand the party as Wawasan Negara.
He said the decision marked a new chapter not only for the party, but also for efforts to strengthen PN as a coalition increasingly trusted by the people to lead the country.
"Wawasan Negara's participation will add value to PN's strength, particularly in advancing an agenda of change anchored on competence, integrity and stability," he said in a statement today.
Yesterday, PN chairman Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Samsuri Mokhtar announced that the coalition had accepted PCM and Parti Pejuang Tanah Air (Pejuang) into PN following an emergency Supreme Council meeting.
Hamzah said Wawasan Negara carried the aspirations of people from various backgrounds and believed the country's future could only be built through cooperation, moderation and commitment to the national interest.
"We are here as a bridge to connect with more Malaysians, expand support and further strengthen PN's position as an inclusive and stable coalition ready to govern," he added.
Yesterday, Perikatan Nasional (PN) accepted
two new political parties
into the coalition following its emergency Supreme Council meeting tonight, says PN chairman Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Samsuri Mokhtar.
He said the two-hour meeting, which focused on PN membership and preparations for the Johor election, agreed to admit Parti Pejuang Tanah Air and Parti Cinta Malaysia into the coalition.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Muda-PSM alliance built on reform, anti-corruption agenda'
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Muda's alliance with Parti Sosialis Malaysia (PSM) under the Progressive Bloc was built on a shared commitment to institutional reform, anti-corruption and the people's economy.
Muda president Amira Aisya Abd Aziz said PSM had been Muda's closest ally since 2023 in pushing for clean institutions and speaking up for ordinary Malaysians.
She said the pact was also founded on shared principles, including the fight against political elites, corporate excess and old-style politics.
"The comrades in PSM have been focused on the agenda of clean institutions and speaking up for the people together with Muda.
"No one can deny their strength in fighting political elites and the ultra-rich," she said in a statement today.
On Thursday, Amira said Muda would contest the Johor and Negri Sembilan elections
under the Progressive Bloc
with PSM.
She said both parties had agreed on a common progressive policy platform, while remaining open to discussions with other opposition parties that shared similar principles.
Amira said institutional reform, climate and environmental issues, and the people's economy would be the bloc's main pillars.
"The Progressive Bloc will also focus on fighting political parties that defend corruption, old political practices and outdated politics.
"I believe this is the start of a bigger future based on pro-people policies in Johor and Malaysia.
"Muda and PSM are determined to contest, win and bring this people's voice into the Johor state assembly," she said.
In the 15th General Election, Muda had cooperated with Pakatan Harapan and later supported the formation of the unity government.
However, the party
withdrew its support
for the government bloc in September 2023 following the discharge not amounting to an acquittal (DNAA) granted to Umno president and Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man seriously injured in alleged stabbing by wife in Kota Samarahan
Article:
KUCHING: A woman has been arrested on suspicion of stabbing her husband with a knife during an altercation at a house in Jalan Datuk Mohammad Musa, Kota Samarahan, on Monday.
Acting Kota Samarahan district police chief Deputy Superintendent Barry William said the 35-year-old victim suffered severe abdominal injuries in the 6.50pm incident.
He said police were alerted to the incident by hospital authorities, who reported treating a man believed to have been stabbed by his wife.
"Initial investigations revealed that the couple has been married for 12 years and has three children.
"However, they have been living separately for over two years due to marital misunderstandings.
"Our investigations also indicate that an argument erupted between them before the incident, which is believed to have been triggered by jealousy," he said in a statement today.
He said the victim had reportedly gone to the suspect's rented house and struck her on the head with a motorcycle helmet, suspecting her of having an affair with another man.
Following the assault, the 31-year-old suspect, who sustained head injuries, allegedly grabbed a knife and stabbed her husband as he followed her into the house.
The altercation was witnessed by the suspect's mother, as the victim was on a WhatsApp video call with her when the incident occurred.
"The suspect claimed she acted in self-defence after being assaulted by the victim," Barry said.
He said the suspect subsequently rushed her husband to the Sarawak Heart Centre in Kota Samarahan for immediate medical attention.
The victim sustained severe abdominal wounds, with part of his intestines protruding, and had to undergo emergency surgery.
However, he did not suffer serious injuries to other internal organs and is currently recovering under hospital observation.
Police have seized a knife believed to have been used in the assault.
The suspect has been remanded to assist in investigations under Section 326 of the Penal Code for voluntarily causing grievous hurt by dangerous weapons or means.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Health Ministry committed to sustaining private clinic operations - Dzulkefly
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Health Ministry (MOH) is committed to ensuring the sustainability of private clinic operations through outsourcing arrangements to enable them to remain competitive in the market.
Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad said such intervention measures are critical to building a healthy healthcare ecosystem, while safeguarding the welfare of private medical practitioners.
"I am acutely aware of this issue and dealt with the closure of private general practitioner (GP) clinics firsthand, particularly during the Covid-19 pandemic.
"We want to ensure that they not only survive but can thrive and continue providing services, as they fill existing gaps in the healthcare system," he said in reply to a question from Dr Halimah Ali (PN-Kapar) during Ministers' Question Time in the Dewan Rakyat today.
In her supplementary question, Halimah said that the private primary healthcare sector is currently under pressure due to a decline in the intake of house officers, with 2,034 private medical clinics reportedly having closed since 2013.
To safeguard the sustainability of their operations, Dzulkefly said the government has taken proactive measures by raising the minimum consultation fee for private medical practitioners under existing regulations to RM80, from RM10 previously.
Earlier, in reply to Halimah's original question, Dzulkefly said the network of private GPs is in fact forms part of the backbone of the country's primary healthcare system, serving as the frontline defence.
"The primary healthcare system, comprising 2,916 MOH health clinics and 10,208 private GP clinics, is the backbone and foundation of our basic healthcare system," he said.
As such, he said structured collaboration between the public and private sectors is crucial to balancing the healthcare burden borne by government hospitals, particularly in addressing the growing prevalence of non-communicable diseases (NCDs).
"As we look at the 13th Malaysia Plan (13MP), we have included the aspect of NCD disease management in collaboration between private clinics and MOH health clinics," he said.
In her original question, Halimah asked whether the ministry planned to strengthen the primary healthcare model through closer integration between government clinics and private practitioners in managing NCDs to ease hospital congestion, similar to systems in the United Kingdom and Taiwan.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Monk's RM1 million win a reminder to use money wisely
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: A 73-year-old monk from a temple in Air Itam never expected his modest savings would bring him a RM1 million windfall, saying the money's true value lies in how it is used.
Chan Chai Siang, the second BSN Sijil Simpanan Premium (SSP) millionaire from Penang this year, said he did not save with the intention of winning, but only to keep his money safe.
The monk, who has served at a temple in Air Itam for more than 30 years, said he was shocked when he was informed he had become BSN SSP's 124th millionaire.
"They called three times. At first, I did not believe it. I thought it could not happen," he said during a media interview at Bank Simpanan Nasional Penang main branch here today.
Chan said he only believed the news after BSN representatives personally met him.
He said he started saving with BSN SSP in January 2025 after a friend recommended the scheme, with his deposit initially around RM2,000.
"The money came from ang pow collections. I used some to help others and kept some for rainy days," he added.
The former factory worker, who had worked in Singapore and Penang before becoming a monk in 1985, said the unexpected windfall would not change his lifestyle.
"I have no plans yet. I will put it back in the bank. I will use it when needed," he said.
"Not for playing (gambling). (I will) use (the money) wisely. Money is useful depending on how you use it. If you use it wisely, you can make yourself happy and make others happy. That is where the true meaning lies," he said.
Chan said his priority would be to support people who need help, including possible medical assistance.
He said in modern society, people must also take care of themselves as medical costs can be expensive.
"You must take care of yourself. When you are sick, you need money. If you can help people, you help," he said.
Chan, who was born in Penang said he never won any lucky draws before and never expected to receive a major prize.
"Never thought about it. No lucky draw before. One time only and it became a big prize," he said.
On whether he believed the win was a blessing, Chan said everyone had their own beliefs.
"If you believe, you believe. But you must use it properly," he said.
Chan said he had no relatives to rely on, with only three close siblings remaining, from eight, and the money would help provide security for their future.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Latin America's resurgent right notches another win in Colombia
Article:
POPULIST leaders are sweeping into power across Latin America, with Colombian voters the latest to embrace hardline right-wing politics – but will the trend last?
With the big exceptions of Brazil and Mexico, it is tough to find a presidential palace in Latin America not occupied by a tough-talking or charismatic rightwinger.
The right has won a string of elections from Argentina to Honduras. Still, experts see little evidence for a region-wide ideological shift – rather a favourable playing field for outsider candidates.
What unites the victors, according to far-right politics expert Lisa Zanotti, is their ability to tap into grievance, build a strong personal brand and forge ad hoc coalitions.
El Salvador's Nayib Bukele – who has jailed almost two per cent of the population – may have provided the blueprint.
Offering seemingly simple solutions to crime, economic stagnation and a discredited political elite, he is astronomically popular at home and often tops polls across the region.
His outsider model was adopted wholesale, including by the similarly coiffed Abelardo de la Espriella, whom a few Colombian commentators have jokingly dubbed the "Temu Bukele."
Latin America's presidential systems can facilitate this trend of outsider candidates, according to Zanotti, a researcher at Budapest's CEU Democracy Institute and Santiago's Ultra-Lab.
"Presidential elections allow political entrepreneurs to bypass weak or discredited parties and construct a direct relationship with voters," she said.
"In the 2000s we saw 'pink tide' governments in Latin America" with the left winning many elections," said Anthony Pereira of Tulane University, who sees populism as an evolving tradition in Latin America.
The left won through populist cash transfers, raising minimum wages, helping people go from under-the-table jobs to formal ones with actual contracts, and granting access to credit and education – all paid for by rising commodity prices.
"The number of people living in poverty went down" he said. Then came the commodities crash and "the optimism of the 2010s turned to disappointment and resentment."
At the same time, "organised crime grew stronger and ran entire neighbourhoods – and prisons. Voters started to respond more enthusiastically to politicians who claimed to be anti-establishment," he said.
De la Espriella appealed not just to anti-left voters, but to a burgeoning middle class frustrated by growing crime – a leading issue for voters in every recent Latin American election.
While the left has struggled to formulate a response, the right has promised quick answers – from airstrikes to megajails.
Much of the world still thinks of Latin American organised crime in terms of cut-throat cocaine cartels and cavalier capos like Pablo Escobar.
In reality, from Ecuador to Brazil, these groups – while still violent – have become multi-million and even multi-billion-dollar conglomerates.
[[nid:1470731]]
Instead of simply shipping cocaine to the United States, they now permeate Latin American life – demanding protection money from Peruvian bus drivers, or taking over gold mines in Venezuela's jungles.
"The extortion is too much. Shops are closing because people can't survive like this," said Sandra Gutierrez, a 60-year-old Colombian voter.
"The crime is unbearable."
According to the latest Latinobarometro poll, 75 per cent of Latin Americans surveyed in more than a dozen countries said crime had risen in the last year.
About a third said they or their family members had been personally affected by crime in the same period.
Hard-right governments in the United States, Russia, Israel and Europe have also worked hard to export their model, or find Latin American leaders who share their worldview.
President Donald Trump in particular has tried hard to put his thumb on the scale of several Latin American votes.
In Colombia, he threatened billions of dollars in military aid if De la Espriella's "radical left Marxist" rival won.
The result was Colombia's leftist President Gustavo Petro saw his approval ratings rise. In Sunday's election Petro's protege got 1.5 million more votes than Petro did four years ago.
Brazil's leftist leader Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva also saw his poll numbers rise when Trump threatened sanctions and tried to back his likely rightwing rival in this October's election.
But in office, some of the new rightwing leaders are already seeing the difficulties in meeting expectations.
Bolivia's Rodrigo Paz has faced rolling protests that have caused widespread shortages. Ecuador's Daniel Noboa initially brought down murder rates, which have since rebounded to record highs.
Chile's Jose Antonio Kast has seen his popularity plummet in his first 100 days in office. Now Colombian commentators are wondering whether De la Espriella will suffer a similar fate.
"He campaigned in black and white, sharpening differences," said researcher Juan Alvarez at the Caro y Cuervo Institute.
"Governing is done in shades of gray. What his grey-mode of government will look like, we still don't know."
* The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MACC officers ordered to update asset declarations within one month
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) has instructed all its officers to update their asset declarations within one month as part of efforts to strengthen internal integrity within the agency.
Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said said the move was initiated by the new MACC chief commissioner Datuk Seri Abdul Halim Aman as a lead-by-example approach for the agency's personnel.
She said that under the new leadership, MACC is focusing on restoring its image and public trust through five key principles, namely, uncompromising integrity, fair enforcement, prevention over punishment, technology and transparency; and building public confidence.
"In line with these principles, MACC will continue to act firmly, professionally and free from any political influence or vested interests, based on the MACC Act 2009 and the principle that 'nobody is above the law'," she said in a written parliamentary reply.
She said this in response to a question from Teresa Kok (PH–Seputeh), who asked the Prime Minister about investigations and actions taken by MACC regarding allegations of a "corporate mafia" involving MACC officers and businessmen, as well as reform measures under the new chief commissioner.
The "corporate mafia" issue surfaced in February following an international report by Bloomberg, which alleged the existence of a network of businessmen using influence and assistance from MACC officers to take over shares in several publicly listed companies through coercive or unlawful means.
The report placed MACC's integrity and its then leadership, Tan Sri Azam Baki, under intense public scrutiny.
The revelations sparked strong political pressure, particularly from government coalition parties such as DAP and Bersih, which called for a Royal Commission of Inquiry (RCI) and demanded Azam's resignation. However, both Azam and MACC strongly denied the allegations.
The Cabinet in March instructed enforcement agencies, including the police, to conduct detailed investigations, while a special committee chaired by the Attorney-General was also established.
As of this month, police investigations are still ongoing, with Inspector-General of Police Tan Sri Mohd Khalid Ismail saying there is currently no concrete
evidence linking MACC
to the allegations.
Azalina further said that, in addition to asset declarations, the new MACC leadership is strengthening compliance with standard operating procedures (SOPs), checks and balances, and a zero-tolerance approach to internal misconduct in order to restore institutional credibility.
She said this aligns with the latest Public Service Department circular outlining guidelines on asset ownership, including shares, securities and digital assets among public servants.
MACC is also strengthening compliance with SOPs, financial governance and an integrity-based work culture, while rejecting any form of misconduct that could tarnish the agency's image.
"She said MACC is enhancing the use of modern technology, data analytics, transaction monitoring and strategic intelligence, while expanding cooperation with domestic and international agencies."
Under the new leadership, MACC would act with stronger enforcement, fairer justice and zero compromise against abuse of power, regardless of rank, position or influence, she said.
She said emphasis is being placed on prevention and anti-corruption education, strengthening whistleblower protection, and promoting a disciplined, humble and open organisational culture.
"These comprehensive measures aim to ensure that MACC is seen by the public as an independent, transparent, professional and credible institution, thereby restoring public confidence in the nation's fight against corruption.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Meth-fuelled motorcyclist crashes into hospital's glass doors
Article:
KUALA TERENGGANU: A 28-year-old motorcyclist, who later tested positive for methamphetamine, sustained minor injuries after crashing through the glass doors of the Sultanah Nur Zahirah Hospital emergency unit.
District police chief Assistant Commissioner Azli Mohd Noor said the rider lost control of his vehicle while entering the hospital's Green Zone at 3am, before ploughing into the glass doors.
Azli said the man suffered light injuries to both hands and was treated at the emergency unit.
"At present, the rider is still receiving follow-up treatment in the hospital ward due to his injuries," he said.
The case is being investigated under Rule 10 of the Road Traffic Rules for failure to control a vehicle, which carries a maximum RM300 fine.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 1,059 hiking accidents, 63 fatalities recorded since 2021 - Syed Ibrahim
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A total of 1,059 accidents involving hiking activities, including 63 fatalities and 87 injuries, were recorded nationwide between 2021 and 2025, the Dewan Rakyat was told today.
Deputy Minister of Natural Resources and Environmental Sustainability Syed Ibrahim Syed Noh said statistics from the Fire and Rescue Department of Malaysia highlighted the need to strengthen safety measures amid the growing popularity of hiking and forest recreational activities in recent years.
To ensure that recreational activities in Permanent Reserved Forests are managed systematically, he said the Peninsular Malaysia Forestry Department, with funding support from the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), had developed the Mountain Risk Assessment and Management Guideline (Mogram) as a technical reference for risk mitigation and the management of hiking trail carrying capacity.
"The Forestry Department has also made it mandatory to engage Forestry Mountain Guides (MGP) in 189 high-risk hiking areas. These certified guides support hiker safety, emergency response and compliance with hiking practices.
"To date, 2,322 individuals from local communities, including indigenous communities, have been certified as MGPs through the MGP skills development programme," he said during Ministers' Question Time in the Dewan Rakyat today.
He was responding to a question from Datuk Mohd Isam Mohd Isa (BN-Tampin) on the number of accidents and fatalities linked to hiking activities over the past five years, as well as the government's efforts to establish a national safety standard for hiking trails nationwide.
Elaborating, Syed Ibrahim said the ministry, through the department, is developing a hiking trail management system based on geospatial technology, geographic information systems (GIS) and remote sensing, in collaboration with the Malaysian Space Agency (MySA).
He said the system would support the mapping and monitoring of hiking trails, centralised management of trail information, and enhance search and rescue (SAR) operations through more accurate and comprehensive spatial analysis.
At present, he said, the registration of hiking activities in HSKs is carried out either manually or through online platforms managed by state forestry departments, in line with the jurisdiction of state governments, which are responsible for issuing permits to enter such areas.
"However, the ministry is of the view that establishing a national digital hiking registration log system has the potential to strengthen the safety, monitoring and management of hiking activities," he said.
"Such a system could help record hikers' movements more systematically, speed up the tracing process in the event of an emergency, and support the implementation of SAR operations more effectively," he said.
Meanwhile, Syed Ibrahim said the ministry also places serious emphasis on the skills and welfare of MGPs and nature guides, who serve on the front line in ensuring the safety of hikers.
He added that various training and certification programmes are conducted regularly, covering hiking safety, risk management, first aid, survival skills, and search and rescue techniques.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fatal 6-vehicle crash: Trailer driver died after being thrown onto road
Article:
KULAI: A trailer driver was killed after being thrown from his vehicle in a six-vehicle crash that sparked a major fire and crippled traffic along the North-South Expressway here today.
A Johor Fire and Rescue Department spokesman said the 9.02am incident involved two trailers, a Toyota Hilux, a Nissan Vanette, a 10-tonne lorry transporting tiles and a one-tonne lorry.
One of the trailers was a Dongfeng model, while the other was a 40-foot vehicle laden with metal fencing.
The spokesman said four of the vehicles were destroyed after catching fire in the collision, which paralysed southbound traffic for several hours.
Firefighters only reached the scene an hour after receiving the distress call due to severe highway congestion.
The trailer driver, a man in his 30s, was pronounced dead at the scene, while four others escaped unharmed.
"However, the condition of the Dongfeng trailer and Nissan lorry drivers are unknown as they were not at the scene when we arrived," the spokesman said.
The operation wrapped up three hours later.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Six men arrested after temple machete attack in Jelutong leaves two victims with severed fingers
Article:
GEORGE TOWN, June 23 — Six men were arrested on suspicions of attacking two men and causing severe injuries at a temple in Jelutong early Sunday morning.
Penang police chief Datuk Dennis Lim Kwang Keng said the 4.30am attack was believed to have been caused by a misunderstanding between a victim and a group of suspects at an entertainment outlet, who then followed the victim to the temple.
The first victim, 31, was accused of stealing one of the suspects mobile phone, but the accusation was untrue.
“The suspects are believed to have followed the victim to a temple in Jelutong before attacking him. Another man, 64, believed to be the temple’s caretaker tried to break up the fight but was set upon by the suspects instead,” he said in a media conference at the Penang contingent police headquarters here today.
The first victim lost his index finger while the second victim lost his little finger in the attack, and both victims suffered injuries from being slashed but are reported to be in stable condition at Penang Hospital, he added.
Following the attack, six men, 26 to 38, were arrested on the same day around Timur Laut district, and a machete, clothes believed to have been worn during the attack, six mobile phones, two keys, a remote control device, two motorcycles and a car were seized.
Preliminary investigations revealed that some suspects had prior drug and criminal records, but all tested negative for drugs, he said, adding that they have been remanded for seven days from yesterday till June 28 to assist investigations under Section 326 and Section 120B of the Penal Code.
In other developments, Lim said 12 men were arrested for suspected football betting and online gambling and RM8,692 in cash was seized in an operation launched in conjunction with the 2026 Fifa World Cup from June 11 to July 19.
The men, 27 to 72, were processed and investigated and has yet to be released on police bail, he said, adding that investigations were being carried out under Section 6(1) and 6(3) of the Betting Act 1953, Section 4(1)(c) of the Common Gaming Houses Act 1953, and the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act 2001. — Bernama

Title: Altantuya family’s judicial review fixed Oct 6, seeks police action on Azilah sworn statements
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, June 23 — The High Court today set Oct 6 to hear the judicial review application by the family of the late Altantuya Shaariibuu for the police to investigate the additional affidavit and sworn statement of a former police officer involved in the murder of the Mongolian model.
Lawyer Sangeet Kaur Deo, who represents the late father, Dr Shaariibuu Setev, when contacted today, said the case would be heard before Judge Norliza Othman.
On Oct 16, 2025, Judge Datuk Aliza Sulaiman (now a Court of Appeal Judge) allowed Shaariibuu to commence judicial review proceedings after the court was informed that the Attorney-General did not object to the application.
Shaariibuu filed the application naming the Inspector-General of Police, the director of the Criminal Investigation Department, the Royal Malaysian Police, the Attorney-General and the Malaysian Government as respondents.
